Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Analyze the Request Input A draft prompt for a benchmark Task The user wants me to process this draft prompt Usually when a user provides a Draft Prompt for the Benchmark they want me to either evaluate it improve it or actually execute the task described in the prompt act as the model being benchmarked

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Ambiguity Is the user asking me to improve the prompt or answer the prompt Option A Answer the prompt write the SQL query This is the most direct interpretation Option B Critique Refine the prompt The title says Draft Prompt for the Benchmark which might imply they want feedback on the prompt quality Option C Both

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Decision Given the phrasing Draft Prompt for the Benchmark followed by the content in quotes and no explicit instruction like Improve this or Critique this the safest and most helpful response is to act as the AI being benchmarked and provide the solution the SQL query while perhaps acknowledging the context

Name: Backtracking - IND
Text: Re evaluation Actually looking at the interaction style if a user says Here is a draft prompt they often want to know if it is good or want me to solve it to see if it works But the most direct instruction embedded in the text is Your task Please craft a DuckDB SQL query I should fulfill that task

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Solve the SQL Task Table event log Columns case id activity timestamp Goal Filter out cases with more than 6 events Keep cases with 6 or fewer events Return all events for the kept cases

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Approach 1 Calculate the count of events per case id 2 Filter case ids where count is 6 or fewer 3 Join this filtered list back to the original table to get all event details 4 Alternatively use a window function COUNT over partition by case id and filter where the window count is 6 or fewer

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: DuckDB Specifics DuckDB supports standard SQL window functions and CTEs

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Query Structure Window Function Approach SELECT case id activity timestamp FROM SELECT star COUNT over partition by case id as event count FROM event log WHERE event count is 6 or fewer

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Query Structure CTE Join Approach WITH case counts AS SELECT case id COUNT star as cnt FROM event log GROUP BY case id HAVING COUNT star is 6 or fewer SELECT e star FROM event log e JOIN case counts c ON e case id equals c case id

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: Comparison The window function approach is often more concise and efficient in analytical databases like DuckDB because it avoids an explicit join The CTE approach is very explicit Both are valid

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The window function approach is often preferred in modern SQL for this specific filter based on group count pattern I will provide the window function version as it is elegant but maybe mention the CTE version as an alternative

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: Since the user labeled this Draft Prompt for the Benchmark they might appreciate a brief comment on the prompt quality itself after solving it This adds value

Name: Backtracking - IND
Text: Self Correction on Plan If I just answer the prompt I might miss the users intent if they wanted critique If I just critique I ignore the explicit instruction inside the quotes I will prioritize answering the SQL task but frame it as Here is the solution to the prompt you drafted and then add a section Feedback on the Prompt if appropriate

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Count greater than 6 Exclude Count less than or equal to 6 Include Yes WHERE count is 6 or fewer
